what_it_does: This noticeboard item enumerates six capabilities a real annotator expects from an annotation/review system and implies those capabilities are currently missing.
observations: Frontmatter follows the noticeboard item schema with id, address, type=block, source=claude_code, state=current, channel=dragnet-development, and a part_of link to item-a72b23c2.
Content is a numbered Markdown list of prescriptive annotation-model requirements.
Lists verb-first marks (Change/Query/Note/Approve/Strike) as gesture types.
Distinguishes inline vs. margin as two semantic registers, with inline carrying a proposed diff.
Calls out disambiguation via verbatim anchored quotes and orphan-detection on change.
Defines passes as resolution boundaries with a pass-level focus note and a submit screen as pass review.
Describes resolution state semantics: open/closed, who-may-resolve, reopen, resolved-recedes, modeled as a fold of the thread.
Mentions stet/reject as two-level priority with structured answer slots, cross-node ranges, and global house-style marks.
standouts: Enumerates six concrete annotation-model requirements rather than describing an implementation.
Frames inline vs. margin as distinct semantic registers.
Models resolution state as a fold of the thread.
Includes stet/reject two-level priority and cross-node ranges.
conventions: Follows the channel-memory noticeboard item format with YAML frontmatter, id, address, links, timestamps, and history block.
Uses a Markdown numbered list with bold topic labels.
Breaks from typical code/design substrate files by being a requirements/gap-analysis note rather than code or structured design artifact.
concerns: The 'P9' prefix in the title is unexplained.
'Tim closes' is mentioned without defining who Tim is or the basis of resolution authority.
The critique of what the system 'won't find' is implied but no baseline or current feature set is described.
notes: A dragnet-development noticeboard item that captures six prescriptive annotation-model requirements and the gaps a real annotator would perceive.
questions: Which specific tool or system is being evaluated as lacking these features?
What does the 'P9' prefix in the title signify?
Who is Tim and what determines their authority to close resolutions?
How do 'passes' map to existing workflow or batch boundaries?
purpose_vs_actual: matches — the stated purpose is to summarize what a real annotator expects and won't find, and the file does exactly that through a numbered list of expected annotation capabilities.
intention_signals: It appears to be advocating for a richer annotation and review model by defining gesture types, semantic registers for inline and margin comments, pass-level boundaries, resolution lifecycle rules, and editorial priority levels.
novelty: Distinct in framing annotation actions as verb-first routed gestures and treating inline vs. margin comments as two separate semantic registers, plus modeling resolution state as a fold of the thread.
contribution: This file contributes a concise, enumerated set of annotation-model requirements and user-expectation signals that a fused system would keep as design constraints for annotation gestures, pass management, resolution lifecycle, and editorial priority handling.
summary_for_embedding: A noticeboard item listing six prescriptive requirements for an annotation system, including verb-first marks, inline/margin registers, disambiguation, passes, resolution states, and stet/reject priorities.
intention_for_embedding: To capture what a real annotator expects from an annotation tool and highlight features perceived as missing.
